author: @Mr_Blinky
text:
Yes, please make a build video, or at least a teardown of what you have built. In all honesty I won't be building one myself as I have a fairly decent hotplate stirrer already. But I am interested in your build, techniques and materials used. I make my own stuff, projects, tools, all sorts and I very much enjoy watching other peoples' builds and teardowns. I find them very interesting and informative.

I can pretty much guess that the stirrer (motor/magnets etc) is based on or similar, or even parts of the one that Rob made some time back using 'builders board'. The heater is interesting though being low voltage. I would really like to see how the heater performs and it's longevity at a high temp. I can't be sure, but it looked like the stirrer bar wasn't located centrally to the motor spindle and looked to be going in circles slightly as opposed to spinning in circles on one point. If so then check that your magnets (more the magnetic field(s) are located centrally to the motor spindle. If so then a way to set them up would be to use it as it is setup but with an empty beaker and somehow move the magnets about until the bar is literally just spinning on one spot. I don't know how easy that would be to do as I don't know your build, but should be doable. That is if necessary.

Keep up the good work, I look forward to the next video :)

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text:
Thanks mate!
I will be doing a follow up video in the next day or so. 
I will defiantly be doing a tear down for the people who want to see!

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
I made my own awhile back by removing the fins off a larger computer fan and attaching magnets to the top.  
I used an old computer power supply to power the motor.  I guess i can add a buck converter to slow it down if i want. 

I heated milk jug plastic (HDPE ) scraps in my oven to melt and encase a piece of iron to make my magnetic stirrer bar. 

I didnt make a hot plate. But if i do, i think

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text:
hey that is awesome!
This was the route I was going to go with the motor,
I ended up using a motor from Vcr. which worked pretty well.
I still a big computer fan with the fins removed so i might do a comparison.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey diy often comes down to using what you have, and E-waste from older computers for a power supply and motor, and milk jug plastic is chemical pretty inert and available to most everyone. 😊

author: @77pete7
text:
Nice job getting this to work, Luke. If you want the beaker to heat quicker, you could a) insulate the beaker with something fireproof!** and b) assuming that the plate is heated from its underside, maybe use some sort of heat conducting paste to ensure a better contact of the heating plate to the beaker. 

** I have learnt a little since my A level days where I had to heat some paraffin for an experiment, and I was caught doing it over a bunsen burner! (Then there was the time I tried to fry sausages in paraffin... but that wasn’t really my fault, since our leader had decided to use a cooking oil tin to bring the fuel for the stove! Yes, the sausages were inedible.)

When giving performance data, it is simpler to just give the number of minutes from the time you switch on, rather than the time of day. Also, a graph of temperature plotted against time gives a far more clear picture of what is going on.

Lastly, you overuse the word ambient. Ambient means the surroundings ... ie ambient temperature is the room temperature, which is certainly a factor (unless you have everything perfectly insulated to stop all heat loss). Then the temperature of the solution is simply the temperature of the solution ... not the ambient temperature of the solution.

Good luck with further developments!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text:
Hi @77pete7,
Thank you for the advice!!
I'm defiantly thinking about the conducting paste idea, I think this could work with our ink, which would be awesome!
Either way I will make a follow up in the next couple of days, hopefully using the word "ambient" less lol.


Thanks mate!

author: @timcollins1131
text: Tip; before you post a video, build a storyboard, prepare a script and have at least one rehearsal -that way you can make sure your video is 100% watchable and you don't repeat the inane yank word "'awesome"

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
Your tip is only partially good.

A story board may not be what he needs. He may only need bullet points to keep him on track.

As far as his repeating the yank word awesome; that is part of his personality. If he attempts to change that he will cease to be authentic and cease to be himself. That in the long run would reduce his viewership.

The people any creator attracts are those for whom the message is for. The best part of this rule is that no one has to try and be anything other than themselves on camera. So what if he is nervous and skips around a bit. It's not like he's been doing this for as long as Rob has.

What he really needs to do is ignore people like you who clearly do not know what they are talking about as they demand a certain perfectionist format and just take the time he needs to improve his video making craft by making videos and uploading them.

As far as awesome is concerned, your just jealous that awesome is awesome and your not awesome!

If you don't like him talking about his friend "awesome" so much, then don't watch his videos. After all, his friend awesome is: well, awesome.

replies.author: @Mr_Blinky
replies.text: All he needs to do, is more of what he is doing. He is young, energetic, likeable, seems to know what he is talking about and lacks nothing more than experience of putting himself in front of an audience. 'Polished' youtubers tend to have a fair few videos that have taken countless hours to create. I'd rather have good content that looks rough, rather than rough content that looks good.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@Mr_Blinky 
That is the same advice every honest, successful; YouTuber gives. 😎👍

replies.author: @scotttovey
